title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 8 (Fragment 3)
text:
“A mean trick with an amplifying spell,” Angelica said. She picked up the candlestick and looked at it with disgust. The branched part was bent at right angles to the base. “What is this place?” she said. “Everything’s so shoddy.”

They got up and went to the windows again, in hopes of a clue. Several little spire-shaped trees were clearly to be seen, just outside, and a sort of terrace beyond that. But, peer as they might, all they could make out further off was queer blue distance, with one or two square-shaped mountains catching the sun on a glossy corner or so. There seemed to be no sky.

“It’s a spell,” said Angelica. Her voice suggested she might be going to panic again. “A spell to stop us knowing where we are.”

Tonino supposed it must be. There was no other way of accounting for the strange absence of view. “But I’m sure I know,” he said, “by those trees. We’re in one of those rich villas by the Palace.”

“You’re right,” agreed Angelica. The panic had left her voice. “I shall never envy those people again. Their lives are all show.”

They turned from the windows and discovered that the vast banging had dislodged one of the wall panels behind the dining table. It hung open like a door. They shoved one another out of the way to reach it first. But there was only a cupboard-sized bathroom, without a window.

“Good,” said Angelica. “I was wondering what we’d do. And at least we’ll have water.” She reached out to one of the taps over the small washbasin. It came away in her hand. Under it was a blob of glue on white china. It was clear the tap had never been meant to be used. Angelica stared at it with such a ridiculous look of bewilderment that Tonino laughed. She drew herself up at that. “Don’t you laugh at me, you beastly Montana!” She stalked out into the main room and threw the useless tap on to the table with a clump. Then she sat in one of the two remaining chairs and rested her elbows gloomily on the table.

After a while, Tonino did the same. The chair creaked under him. So did the table. Though its surface was painted to look like smooth mahogany, close to, it was all blobs of varnish and huge splinters. “There’s nothing that’s not shoddy,” he said.

“Including you, Whatyoumecall Montana!” Angelica said. She was still angry.

“My name’s Tonino,” Tonino said.

“It’s the last twist of the knife, being shut up with a Montana!” Angelica said. “Whatever your name is. I shall have to put up with all your filthy habits.”

“Well, I’ve got to put up with yours,” Tonino said irritably. It suddenly struck him that he was all alone, far from the friendly bustle of the Casa Montana. Even when he was hidden in a corner of the Casa with a book, he knew the rest of the family was all around him. And Benvenuto would be purring and pricking him, to remind him he was not alone. Dear old Benvenuto. Tonino was afraid he was going to cry—in front of a Petrocchi too. “How did they catch you?” he said, to take his mind off it.

“With a book.” A slight, woeful smile appeared on Angelica’s tight white face. “It was called The Girl Who Saved Her Country , and I thought it was from Great-Uncle Luigi. I still think it was a good story.” She looked defiantly at Tonino.

Tonino was annoyed. It was not pleasant to think he had been caught by the same spell as a Petrocchi. “Me too,” he said gruffly.

“And I haven’t got any filthy habits!” snapped Angelica.

“Yes you have. All the Petrocchis have,” said Tonino. “But I expect you don’t realize because they’re normal to you.”

“I like that!” Angelica picked up the broken tap, as if she had half a mind to throw it.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being not present or not existing.
vocabulary.example: There was no other way of accounting for the strange absence of view.

vocabulary.definition: To explain or be the reason for something.
vocabulary.example: There was no other way of accounting for the strange absence of view.

vocabulary.definition: A magical phrase or formula intended to increase the effect of something.
vocabulary.example: A mean trick with an amplifying spell.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of being confused or puzzled.
vocabulary.example: She stared at it with such a ridiculous look of bewilderment.

vocabulary.definition: Busy, energetic, and noisy activity.
vocabulary.example: The friendly bustle of the Casa Montana.

vocabulary.definition: A piece of evidence or information used in the detection of a problem or mystery.
vocabulary.example: They went to the windows again, in hopes of a clue.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing open resistance or bold disobedience.
vocabulary.example: She looked defiantly at Tonino.

vocabulary.definition: A strong feeling of dislike or disapproval, often caused by something unpleasant or offensive.
vocabulary.example: She looked at it with disgust.

vocabulary.definition: To force out of a fixed position.
vocabulary.example: The vast banging had dislodged one of the wall panels.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of discontented or resentful longing aroused by someone else's possessions or qualities.
vocabulary.example: I shall never envy those people again.

vocabulary.definition: Unpleasant or disgusting habits or behaviours.
vocabulary.example: I shall have to put up with all your filthy habits.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows sadness or lack of hope.
vocabulary.example: She rested her elbows gloomily on the table.

vocabulary.definition: Having a shiny and smooth surface.
vocabulary.example: Mountains catching the sun on a glossy corner.

vocabulary.definition: In a rough or stern manner of speaking.
vocabulary.example: He said gruffly.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden overwhelming fear causing irrational behaviour.
vocabulary.example: Her voice suggested she might be going to panic again.

vocabulary.definition: Strange or odd; unusual.
vocabulary.example: All they could make out was queer blue distance.

vocabulary.definition: Of poor quality or workmanship.
vocabulary.example: Everything’s so shoddy.

vocabulary.definition: An ostentatious display intended to impress others.
vocabulary.example: Their lives are all show.

vocabulary.definition: Having the shape of a spire, a tapering conical or pyramidal structure on buildings.
vocabulary.example: Several little spire-shaped trees were clearly to be seen.

vocabulary.definition: A small, thin, sharp piece broken off from a larger object.
vocabulary.example: Huge splinters on the table.

vocabulary.definition: To walk stiffly or angrily.
vocabulary.example: She stalked out into the main room.

vocabulary.definition: A level paved area or platform next to a building; or a flat area of ground next to a building for sitting or walking.
vocabulary.example: A sort of terrace beyond that.

vocabulary.definition: An action or comment that makes a painful or unpleasant situation worse.
vocabulary.example: It’s the last twist of the knife, being shut up with a Montana!

vocabulary.definition: A liquid coating that dries to form a hard, shiny surface protecting or decorating wood or other materials.
vocabulary.example: The table was all blobs of varnish and huge splinters.

vocabulary.definition: Expressing sorrow or misery.
vocabulary.example: A slight, woeful smile appeared.
